answer:
**The work is *Andreas* by Cynewulf (an anonymous Anglo-Saxon poet who signed his name in runes), Chapter 1.**[^1]

This Old English poem stages the described dialogue at sea between Saint Andrew (Andreas) and the disguised Christ, who serves as the helmsman of their miraculously calm ship en route to rescue Matthew from the Mermedonians. Andrew praises the helmsman (still unrecognized as the Lord of Glory) for his extraordinary skill, noting his youth ("young, shelter of warriors, not old in winters"), calling him a "wave-rider," describing the ship as "foam-necked" and faring "most like a fowl," and confessing he has been "'sixteen times on a sea-boat' yet [has] never seen such a steersman."

Here is Andrew's full praise (the concluding speech of his dialogue with the helmsman):

> “Never have I met a better seaman,
> more skilled in his craft, so it seems to me,
> a stronger rower, a shrewder guide,
> wiser in his words. I wish to ask you,
> uncorrupted hero, for one more boon,
> though I have few rings, or rich treasures,
> or hammered gold to hand you now.
> I would win your friendship, glorious chieftain,
> your good will, if I could but gain it.
> For this you will reap a reward,
> a holy hope in heaven’s high glory,
> if you will grant your grace and your lore
> to us, the sea-weary. I wish to seek from you,
> battle-brave hero, one certain skill:
> that you would teach me, since the true King
> and Shaper of men gave you such strength,
> how you hold the wave-floater steady,
> the sea-stallion, and steer it on the sound.
> I have been, in my time, now and before,
> **sixteen times on a sea-boat**,
> my hands frozen while stirring the mere,
> the ocean-streams—this is one time more—
> yet I have never seen any man,
> any son of heroes, who is like to you,
> steering at the stern. The stream’s-welling stills,
> the surf beats the shore. This boat is so swift,
> **it fares, foam-necked, most like a fowl**,
> gliding on the ocean. I know for a fact
> that I have never seen on the wave-path
> in any seafarer such a strange skill.
> It is most like as if it stood still
> on the shore of the land, where no storm can stir it,
> no wind can awaken it, no water-floods
> break its broad prow, yet on the brine it speeds,
> swift under sail. **You yourself are young**,
> **shelter of warriors, not old in winters**,
> **yet you have in your heart, you wave-rider**,
> the answer of a hero. You know for every word
> in the world its wise understanding.”[^2]

[^1]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, [Chapter 1](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/).
[^2]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-ca9772).
